critic_raw: The hybrid CNN-LSTM vs. logistic regression experiment for readmission prediction in chronic disease patients is structurally sound but has several methodological concerns. First, the 1D CNN is applied to temporal sequences of visit dynamics, and as with other CNN-on-sequences designs, the translational invariance assumption is problematic for clinical event sequences where the absolute timing of events matters. A clinical deterioration at day 150 post-admission carries different meaning than at day 15, and the CNN's local convolutions do not naturally model absolute position. Second, the success criterion specifies 'meaningfully higher AUROC (≥0.03 absolute improvement)' as the threshold for supporting the hypothesis. This threshold is arbitrary and underpowered without a statistical power analysis—0.03 absolute AUROC may or may not be statistically significant depending on test set size and class imbalance. With ~2,500 test patients and 15-25% readmission rate, a 0.03 AUROC difference may fall inside confidence intervals. Third, the logistic regression baseline is trained on the identical feature set as the CNN-LSTM input. If the features are designed for temporal modeling (e.g., rolling 30/60/90-day admission counts), logistic regression on these features may actually capture much of the relevant signal without needing sequential modeling, making the comparison fair but potentially showing minimal difference. Fourth, the design uses 180-day lookback features as static engineered inputs for logistic regression—but the CNN-LSTM processes these as temporal sequences. This means the same underlying data is represented differently for the two models; logistic regression loses the ordering information. This is an acknowledged part of the comparison but worth surfacing explicitly.
all_issues_raised: 1D CNN translational invariance assumption invalid for clinical event sequences with positional meaning
0.03 AUROC success threshold is arbitrary and not backed by statistical power analysis
Temporal features given to logistic regression may capture much of the sequential signal without sequential modeling
Same data represented differently (temporal sequence vs. static aggregates) for the two models

round_transcripts.critic: The CNN-LSTM vs. logistic regression experiment has concerns: (1) 1D CNN translational invariance assumption is invalid for clinical sequences; (2) the 0.03 AUROC success threshold lacks statistical power analysis; (3) temporal engineered features may give logistic regression enough sequential information; (4) the same underlying data is represented as sequences vs. static aggregates for the two models, which is the hypothesis design but should be acknowledged as a confound.
round_transcripts.defender: On (1): The CNN extracts local patterns from the temporal sequence which are then fed to the LSTM that does model temporal ordering. The combined architecture addresses the stationarity concern—the CNN operates at local scale, the LSTM at global scale. This is a standard and validated design. On (2): The 0.03 threshold reflects clinical practice standards for meaningful model improvement; statistical testing is also specified (evaluation via AUROC on the test set). A power analysis should be added to confirm whether ~2,500 test patients detect 0.03 absolute AUROC. On (3): The comparison is explicitly designed to test whether temporal architecture adds value over aggregated features—if logistic regression performs nearly as well, that is an informative finding. On (4): The feature representation difference is the hypothesis; the design correctly surfaces this distinction.

round_transcripts.critic: On (2): The defense concedes that a power analysis should be added; this is a necessary specification for the success criterion to be meaningful. On (1): The LSTM models temporal ordering after CNN feature extraction, but the CNN's feature extraction stage may produce misleading local patterns for irregular sequences before they reach the LSTM. This is a design risk. On (3): If logistic regression on temporal aggregates performs similarly, the experiment may not provide evidence for the hypothesis—reporting a null result is valid, but the power to detect a meaningful difference must be pre-specified.
round_transcripts.defender: On (2): A formal power analysis is the right addition and would specify whether 2,500 test patients suffice to detect 0.03 AUROC at appropriate alpha/beta. This is a design completion item. On (1): Practical evidence from clinical time series literature supports CNN-LSTM combinations; the theoretical concern is real but empirically mitigated in practice. On (3): Null results are valid and informative; the design is appropriate whether or not the hypothesis is supported.

all_issues_adjudicated: 0.03 AUROC success threshold lacks statistical power analysis; unclear if test set provides sufficient power
1D CNN applied to irregular clinical sequences before LSTM may introduce misleading local pattern extraction
